class_schedule.topic: The Writer's Workshop: Process and Practice
learning_objectives: Understand the workshop model for creative writing feedback
Distinguish between craft-focused feedback and personal opinion
Establish a daily writing practice with journaling techniques
readings: Lamott, Bird by Bird, Ch. 1-3 ('Getting Started,' 'Short Assignments,' 'Shitty First Drafts')
Workshop guidelines and etiquette handout (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Creative writing is a craft, not a gift. Like any craft, it is developed through practice, study, and honest feedback. This course teaches you the fundamentals of fiction and poetry writing through the workshop model.
key_concepts: The workshop model: author silent, peers discuss the work, author responds
Craft-focused feedback: addressing technique, not personal taste
The revision mindset: first drafts are raw material, not finished products
Daily writing practice: building the habit that sustains a writing life
Reading as a writer: studying published work for technique, not just enjoyment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mock workshop of a short published flash fiction piece to demonstrate constructive critique methodology
setup_requirements: Access to course anthology and LMS

review.value: The author remains silent while their work is discussed, then responds briefly; this prevents defensive explanation and forces the writing to speak for itself
Workshop feedback addresses whether the technique achieves its apparent purpose, not whether the reader personally likes the subject matter or style

learning_objectives: Create characters through action, dialogue, and detail rather than description
Develop a distinctive narrative voice appropriate to a story's character and situation
Identify and avoid common characterization pitfalls: stock characters, telling instead of showing
readings: O'Connor, 'A Good Man Is Hard to Find'

lecture_notes.introduction: Characters are not described into existence. They are revealed through what they do, what they say, and what they notice. The writer's job is to put characters under pressure and let the reader draw conclusions.
key_concepts: Characterization through action: what a character does under pressure reveals who they are
Dialogue as characterization: speech patterns, evasions, and contradictions
Telling detail: the one specific detail that reveals character more than a paragraph of description
Interiority: when and how to grant the reader access to a character's thoughts
Flat vs. round characters: both serve legitimate purposes depending on the story
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analysis of O'Connor's grandmother in 'A Good Man Is Hard to Find,' examining how every detail of her preparation for the trip characterizes her without a single line of direct description
setup_requirements: Printed copy of 'A Good Man Is Hard to Find'

review.value: Rather than writing 'She was anxious,' show the anxiety through action and detail: she checked the lock three times, rearranged the silverware, and started sentences she did not finish
A single concrete detail can characterize more efficiently than a paragraph of generalities: 'She kept a laminated list of her enemies in her wallet' reveals personality instantly

learning_objectives: Write setting that functions as more than backdrop by reflecting character and theme
Engage all five senses in descriptive writing rather than relying only on sight
Structure a scene with a clear purpose: something must change by the scene's end

lecture_notes.introduction: Setting is not wallpaper. In effective fiction, setting does work — it reflects mood, constrains or enables action, and carries thematic weight. A scene is not a unit of time; it is a unit of change.
key_concepts: Setting as character: how environment reflects and shapes the people in it
The five senses: moving beyond visual description to sound, smell, touch, taste
Scene structure: goal, conflict, outcome — something must change
Objective correlative: using external details to convey internal emotional states
White space and what to leave out: Hemingway's iceberg theory
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of 'Hills Like White Elephants,' analyzing how Hemingway's sparse setting details — the train station, the hills, the beaded curtain — carry the story's unspoken emotional content
setup_requirements: Printed copy of 'Hills Like White Elephants'

review.value: T.S. Eliot's term for using external objects, situations, or events to evoke a particular emotion in the reader without naming the emotion directly
A scene dramatizes action in real time with dialogue and sensory detail; summary compresses time and tells the reader what happened. Both are necessary, but scenes carry the story's emotional weight.

class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Poetry: Image and Line
learning_objectives: Write concrete images that carry emotional and thematic resonance
Understand the poetic line as a unit of meaning distinct from the sentence
Practice line breaks as a tool for emphasis, surprise, and pacing
readings: Williams, 'The Red Wheelbarrow' and 'This Is Just to Say'
Oliver, A Poetry Handbook, Ch. 3 - 'Imagery' and Ch. 5 - 'The Line'

lecture_notes.introduction: Poetry begins with the image and the line. The image is what the poem shows; the line is how the poem breathes. Before rhythm, before metaphor, before anything else, a poet must learn to see clearly and break lines meaningfully.
key_concepts: The concrete image: specific, sensory, and grounded in the physical world
Image vs. abstraction: 'grief' is abstract; 'the unwashed coffee cup on the nightstand' is an image
The poetic line: a unit of attention that may or may not coincide with a sentence
Line breaks: end-stopped vs. enjambed, and the micro-pause each break creates
The prose poem: what happens when the line is abandoned
lecture_notes.demonstration: Line-break workshop: the same paragraph of prose is broken into lines in three different ways, and the class discusses how each version creates different emphases and meanings

review.value: The early 20th-century movement that demanded direct treatment of the thing, no word that does not contribute, and rhythm composed in the sequence of the musical phrase
Breaking a line mid-phrase creates forward momentum and a moment of suspense; breaking at a natural pause creates closure and emphasis on the final word of the line

learning_objectives: Apply systematic revision strategies to fiction and poetry drafts
Distinguish between revision (re-seeing the work's vision) and editing (correcting surface errors)
Develop a personal revision checklist based on recurring craft weaknesses
readings: Prose, Reading Like a Writer, Ch. 10 - 'Revision'
Two drafts of a published poem showing significant revision (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Writing is rewriting. The first draft discovers what you want to say; revision shapes it into what it needs to be. Professional writers revise extensively — and strategically, not randomly.
key_concepts: Macro revision: structure, character arc, scene order, beginning and ending
Micro revision: sentence-level clarity, word choice, rhythm, cutting excess
The revision hierarchy: address large problems before small ones
Killing your darlings: cutting material that is well-written but does not serve the piece
Workshop feedback integration: how to sort useful criticism from noise
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing two drafts of Elizabeth Bishop's 'One Art' to observe how the published version emerged through seventeen drafts, each one tightening the formal structure and sharpening the emotional revelation
setup_requirements: Draft of student work for in-class revision exercise

review.value: If a sentence, image, or passage calls attention to its own cleverness rather than serving the story or poem, it must go regardless of how much you like it
Revision is structural and conceptual — re-imagining the piece. Editing is surface-level — grammar, spelling, punctuation. Revise first; edit last.

questions.question: Explain the difference between 'showing' and 'telling' in fiction. Rewrite the following sentence using showing: 'He was very angry.'
questions.answer: Telling states an emotion or quality directly ('He was angry'). Showing renders it through action, dialogue, or detail so the reader infers the emotion. Rewrite example: 'He shoved the chair back from the table. The legs shrieked against the tile. He left the room without closing the door.'

rubric.key: Correct distinction between showing and telling
Rewrite uses concrete action/detail, not abstract statement
Rewrite effectively conveys anger without naming it

questions.question: What is an 'objective correlative'? Identify one in 'Hills Like White Elephants' and explain what emotional state it conveys.
questions.answer: An objective correlative is an external object, scene, or event that evokes a particular emotion in the reader. In 'Hills Like White Elephants,' the barren landscape on one side of the tracks and the fertile fields on the other serve as an objective correlative for the couple's unspoken choice between terminating and continuing the pregnancy.

rubric.key: Identifies a specific example from the text
Explains the emotional content the detail conveys

questions.question: Take the following abstract statement and transform it into a concrete poetic image (2-4 lines): 'Time passes quickly.'
questions.answer: Example: 'The milk in the fridge expired / three Tuesdays ago. The calendar / still shows March.' The response must replace the abstraction with a concrete, sensory image that implies the passage of time without stating it directly.

rubric.key: Image is concrete and sensory, not abstract
Image implies time passing without stating it directly
Line breaks are intentional and contribute to meaning

questions.question: What does it mean to 'kill your darlings' in the revision process? Why is this principle important for developing writers?
questions.answer: 'Kill your darlings' means cutting passages, images, or phrases that the writer is personally attached to but that do not serve the piece as a whole. It is important because beginning writers often keep material that showcases their cleverness at the expense of the work's coherence and impact. Learning to cut what you love in service of the whole is a sign of mature craft.

rubric.key: Explains why it matters for developing writers specifically
Distinguishes between quality of the passage and its function in the whole

major_assignments.title: Short Story Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Write two short stories (1,500-2,500 words each) and submit them with revised second drafts. At least one story must be workshopped in class. Include a 500-word craft reflection analyzing your revision process.
objectives: Apply characterization, setting, and scene-writing techniques from course readings
Demonstrate the ability to construct a scene where something changes
Produce substantive revisions informed by workshop feedback
Articulate your craft choices in a reflective essay
deliverables: Two short stories (1,500-2,500 words each), first and revised drafts
Workshop copies distributed to class one week before workshop date
500-word craft reflection analyzing specific revision choices
Annotated workshop feedback received from peers

criteria: Characters are developed through action, dialogue, and detail
Scenes have clear purpose with conflict and change
Setting engages the senses and serves the story's mood or theme
Prose is controlled and purposeful at the sentence level
Revisions address substantive craft issues, not just surface corrections
Workshop feedback is thoughtfully integrated or deliberately rejected with reasoning
Second drafts show meaningful improvement over first drafts
Craft reflection demonstrates awareness of specific strengths and weaknesses
Stories submitted on time for workshop distribution
Peer feedback is specific, constructive, and craft-focused
Author engages productively with feedback received
Participation in others' workshops demonstrates close reading of peers' work
Stories demonstrate command of fundamental fiction craft elements
Substantive revision showing growth between drafts
Thoughtful craft reflection connecting technique to intention

short_story_portfolio.description: Two short stories with revisions and craft reflection

poetry_portfolio.description: Five poems with revisions demonstrating image, line, and form

workshop_participation.description: Quality of feedback given to peers and engagement with the workshop process

quizzes_and_exercises.description: In-class writing exercises and craft terminology quizzes

final_portfolio.description: Revised selection of best work with a reflective craft essay

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory, especially on workshop days. Missing your own workshop session without prior arrangement results in a zero for that submission. More than 3 unexcused absences lower the final grade by one letter.
course_policies.late_submissions: Workshop submissions must be distributed on time; late distribution cancels your workshop slot. Other assignments are penalized 5% per day late.
course_policies.collaboration: All creative work must be original. Workshop feedback is collaborative by nature, but the writing itself must be your own. Cite any sources that directly influence your work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Submitting work written for another course, by another person, or generated by AI constitutes plagiarism. All work must be original to this course.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Word processing software for manuscript preparation, LMS access for submissions, and printing capability for workshop distribution copies.
